model_narrative: We manage a strategic oil reserve over time while the world oil market randomly switches between a normal state and a disrupted state. In each situation we decide, as an occupation measure, how much weight to place on being at each reserve level in each market state and choosing each next reserve level. We also track the present value of expected cost. The objective is to make that present value of expected cost as small as possible.

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the possible levels of the strategic reserve, from empty up to its largest holding

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the state of the world oil market

sets.name: sp
sets.doc: alias of the reserve-level set, used as the current reserve level in the balance equation

sets.name: spp
sets.doc: alias of the reserve-level set, used as the next-period reserve level

sets.name: j
sets.doc: alias of the oil-market-state set, used as the current market state in the balance equation

params.name: b
params.doc: the per-period discount factor, between 0 and 1

params.name: beta
params.doc: the constant right-hand-side level each balance equation must equal; it is the per-state weight tied to the discount factor

params.name: g
params.doc: the U.S. share of world oil demand

params.name: q
params.doc: supply, in million barrels per year

params.name: d
params.doc: the intercept of the demand curve

params.name: k
params.doc: the coefficient of the demand curve

params.name: pn
params.doc: the normal price, in US dollars per barrel

params.name: pr
params.doc: the transition probability of the world oil market from one state to another

params.name: lev
params.doc: the numeric reserve quantity at each reserve level

params.name: dis
params.doc: the disruption size in each market state; zero when normal

params.name: p
params.doc: the oil price when moving from one reserve level to another in a given market state

params.name: c
params.doc: the cost of the action that moves the reserve from level s to level sp in market state i; indexed in the order reserve-from, reserve-to, market-state

params.name: pi
params.doc: the joint transition coefficient: starting from reserve level s and market state i, taking the action toward current reserve level sp and current market state j, the probability of landing at next reserve level spp; zero for transitions that cannot occur

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the occupation measure: a multiple of the joint probability of being at reserve level s in market state i and choosing the action toward reserve level sp

vars.name: pvcost
vars.doc: the present value of expected cost

constraints.name: z[sp_idx,
constraints.intent: Each combination of a current reserve level and a current market state must satisfy a balance equation. Take the total occupation weight placed on that current reserve level and current market state across all choices of next reserve level. From this subtract the discounted inflow of weight that arrives at that current reserve level and market state from every starting reserve level, starting market state, and next reserve level, where each starting situation contributes according to its joint transition coefficient. This balance must equal the fixed per-state right-hand-side level.

constraints.name: z[s_idx,
constraints.intent: When the market is disrupted, the reserve is not allowed to grow. For every pair of a current reserve level and a next reserve level under the disrupted state, the disrupted occupation weight for moving between those two levels, scaled by how much higher the next reserve level sits above the current one in the level ordering, must be no greater than zero. This rules out moves that increase the reserve while the market is disrupted.

constraints.name: pvcost
constraints.intent: The present value of expected cost equals the total cost of every action taken. For each starting reserve level, market state, and chosen next reserve level, multiply the action's cost by its occupation weight and add these up across all such combinations. Set the present value of expected cost equal to that total.

query: The current policy quietly accepts large drawdowns once a disruption hits, sliding the reserve down hard. Couldn't we require that during the disrupted market, at least 40% of each level's mass holds flat at that level? Wouldn't a stickier disrupted-state policy still leave us close to today's expected cost?
